cataloging_subject.label: The Ormulum
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The late-twelfth-century Middle English work attributed to Orm: its dedication, list of planned Gospel texts, verse preface, biblical narratives, and homiletic exposition. This subject is not the autograph manuscript, either nineteenth-century edition, the University of Michigan encoding, or the repository XML file.

catalog_summary.title: The Ormulum
catalog_summary.author: Orm
catalog_summary.region: East Midlands; Bourne Abbey in south Lincolnshire has been proposed

assertion.notes: The verse preface says that the book is named Orrmulum because Orrm made it; modern editions and catalogs commonly regularize the title to Ormulum.

agents.name: Orm
notes: A late-twelfth-century Augustinian canon who names himself in the verse preface and is identified by the Bodleian as both author and principal scribe of the autograph witness.

agents.name: Robert Meadows White
notes: Editor of the 1852 edition whose text, notes, and glossary were revised by Robert Holt in 1878; not the medieval author.

agents.name: Robert Holt
notes: Editor and collator of the 1878 source edition represented by AFW5744.0001.001; not the medieval author.
The work explicitly names Orrm as its maker; this responsibility must not be reassigned to White or Holt because the U-M bibliographic header lists them in AUTHOR elements.

scope.description: White's 1852 edition, retained as the textual, annotational, and lexicographical basis of Holt's 1878 source edition
The revised 1878 edition used by the U-M encoding
Correction of White's text by renewed collation against Bodleian MS Junius 1

date_statements.display: c. 1175; Oxford describes the work as written in the 1160s and 1170s

assertion.notes: The MED bibliography redated its stencil to c.1175 in 2017 after consultation with the OED; the wider normalized range preserves Oxford's less compressed formulation.

notes: This is the Bodleian's date for the autograph manuscript. Because author and principal scribe are the same person, composition and first attestation overlap, but they remain distinct descriptive claims.

places.label: Bourne Abbey, Lincolnshire
notes: A proposed institutional place of composition, not an established fact.
A linguistic region should not be treated automatically as the exact place of composition.

assertion.notes: The Bodleian catalog and MED bibliography attribute this proposal to Malcolm Parkes and mark it as uncertain.

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: The list of 242 planned pericopes and the Latin Gospel lemmata introducing surviving homiletic units

notes: The XML header's single eng language declaration omits both the Middle English code enm and the embedded Latin established by the text and Bodleian metadata.

form_statements.description: Metrical biblical narratives and homilies in unrhymed fifteen-syllable long lines, conventionally printed as paired half-lines. The ancillary Latin pericope list and headings do not change the primary literary form.

subject_statements.label: History of English language, spelling, and sound

summaries.value: A late-twelfth-century Middle English verse homiliary by the Augustinian canon Orm. At his brother Walter's request, Orm renders Gospel readings into English and follows each narrative with explanation intended to support preaching and Christian instruction. The work is especially important for its highly systematic spelling and evidence for Early Middle English pronunciation.

The surviving autograph is both unfinished and extremely lacunose. Its table plans at least 242 biblical narratives followed by homilies; the CME encoding represents an introductory division and 21 numbered surviving units through number 32, with large internal losses and a fragmentary ending. Van Vliet's seventeenth-century partial transcript preserves passages from leaves later lost, but it is not a coequal medieval witness.

extent.description: The local U-M-derived XML encodes 20,531 L elements in 159 LG elements: 342 lines in the dedication, 106 in the verse preface, and 20,083 in the surviving introduction/narrative/homily body. It separately lists 242 planned pericopes and divides the surviving body into 22 XML items (one unheaded introduction plus 21 numbered units). These are metrics of this representation of a lacunose, unfinished work, not the extent of Orm's original plan.

content_parts.label: Dedication to Walter
content_parts.summary: Orm addresses his brother Walter, explains the commission and intended use of the work, describes his method of translating and explaining Gospel readings, and directs future copyists to preserve his spelling and doubled letters.

content_parts.label: List of 242 planned texts
content_parts.summary: A Latin list of Gospel and Acts pericopes planned for treatment in the work; its extent documents the intended program rather than the number of units now surviving.
notes: The list is incomplete at its end in the autograph and does not prove that all 242 planned treatments were composed.

content_parts.label: Verse preface
content_parts.summary: The 106-line preface names the book Orrmulum because Orrm made it and introduces its Gospel-centered spiritual program.

content_parts.label: Surviving biblical narratives and homilies
content_parts.summary: The surviving verse body begins with an unheaded introduction and continues through 21 numbered units represented by Gospel lemmata numbered I, III, VII, XI, XIV–XVI, XVIII–XXVIII, XXX–XXXII. It ends in fragments after the heading for John XXXII.
notes: The source XML labels this entire DIV1 as introduction, but its 22 child divisions contain the surviving narrative and homiletic body; the label is not treated as a work-level genre or structure claim.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/afw5744.xml, HEADER, title pages, dedication, and verse preface.
evidence.summary: The XML identifies U-M item AFW5744.0001.001, its 2006 OCR encoding, the White/Holt 1878 source edition, and the medieval work's self-naming, authorship, commission, audience, and copying instructions.

evidence.citation: Element and structural inspection of the checked-in afw5744 XML, performed 2026-07-11.
evidence.summary: Inspection found 1,104 PB, 8 DIV1, 264 DIV2, 159 LG, and 20,531 L elements. The dedication has 7 LG and 342 L; the list has 242 DIV2; the preface has 1 LG and 106 L; the body has 22 DIV2, 151 LG, and 20,083 L. The body ends fragmentarily after the heading for John XXXII.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, The Ormulum, AFW5744.0001.001.
evidence.locator: Item information, rights, pages, and contents
evidence.summary: The exact item page identifies the 1878 Clarendon Press source, exposes the encoded text and linked pages, and marks the materials public domain with a No Copyright–United States statement.

evidence.citation: Robert Holt, ed., The Ormulum, with the Notes and Glossary of Dr. R. M. White, 2 vols. (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1878), vol. 1, pp. v–vi.
evidence.locator: Preface to the New Edition, vol. 1, pp. v–vi
evidence.summary: Holt states that White first edited the work from the original Bodleian manuscript and that Holt corrected the text by renewed collation with that manuscript while revising and expanding White's notes and glossary.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, MS Junius 1, Digital Bodleian complete object and IIIF manifest.
evidence.summary: The official record identifies Orm as author and principal scribe, dates the autograph 1170–1185, describes its contents, severe lacunae, hand, extent, provenance, and proposed Bourne origin, and supplies a complete digital facsimile.
notes: Digital Bodleian object 90a06f70-880a-4b5b-bd30-798710afff11 is the complete 263-canvas manuscript. The separate object 8d37b5d4-27ec-4da0-8a9e-dc3cba62c212 contains only two older 35mm images and is not used as the complete facsimile link.

evidence.citation: University of Oxford Faculty of English, The Orrmulum: Bodleian MS Junius 1.
evidence.summary: Oxford describes the work as a vast unfinished Gospel translation written by the Augustinian canon Orrm in the 1160s and 1170s to support preaching in English, and confirms that its only manuscript is completely digitized.

evidence.citation: Middle English Compendium Bibliography, Ormulum, BIB2677.
evidence.locator: Manuscripts, editions, external references, and MED title stencils
evidence.summary: The bibliography supplies the standard indexes, records Junius 1 and Lambeth 783 separately, dates the work c.1175, identifies Holt 1878 and Johannesson–Cooper 2023, and explains that Lambeth 783 is van Vliet's partial transcript of Junius 1, including now-lost material.

evidence.citation: Stockholm University Department of English, The Ormulum, announcement of the Johannesson–Cooper edition, 20 November 2023.
evidence.summary: The project describes the work as East Midlands metrical English sermons by the late-twelfth-century priest Orm, surviving in his autograph Junius 1; it says the 2023 edition uses a fresh transcription and recovers all known missing portions from van Vliet's seventeenth-century copy in Lambeth 783.

evidence.citation: Kees Dekker, The Ormulum in the Seventeenth Century: The Manuscript and Its Early Readers, Neophilologus 102 (2018): 257–277.
evidence.locator: Manuscript; transcription sections; verse; spelling and emendations
evidence.summary: Dekker distinguishes Junius 1 from van Vliet's 49 leaves of excerpts and glossaries in Lambeth 783, maps the Ormulum material to ff. 51r–88r, and shows both that the latter preserves now-lost passages and that selected van Vliet alterations in Junius 1 passed into White/Holt.

open_questions.question: Can Orm's institutional setting and place of composition be established more securely than the proposed Bourne Abbey localization?
next_steps: Review Malcolm Parkes's argument and subsequent paleographical, dialectal, and institutional scholarship.
Preserve competing place proposals as separate assertions rather than replacing the current tentative statement.

open_questions.question: How much of the 242-item plan did Orm compose, and how much of the mismatch with the surviving text results from medieval incompletion versus later loss?
next_steps: Consult the 2023 edition's introduction and projected commentary volume for its reconstruction of the work's compositional stages.
Keep planned extent, once-written-but-lost material, and currently extant text as separate quantities.

notes: The U-M source description encodes White and Holt in AUTHOR elements because it describes the 1878 book; the title pages and preface establish them as editors, while the medieval preface and Bodleian catalog establish Orm as author.
The XML's root language declaration eng is not reused mechanically: the work is Middle English (enm) with embedded Latin.
The XML's broad DIV1 TYPE='introduction' label covers the surviving narrative and homiletic body and is treated as an encoding label, not a reliable work-level structural title.

Access and rights for the medieval text, nineteenth-century editions, modern U-M encoding, Bodleian manuscript images, Lambeth transcript, and 2023 edition are kept separate in the linked lineage manifest.
